commentary: The 50th and final sale of the year was conducted today (30 December 2026) following the re-scheduling of sales due to Cyclone Ditwah in late November. On account of a one-day Auction, offerings were restricted to 4.2 M/Kgs, of which Ex-Estate offerings comprised of 0.76 M/Kgs. Overall, a bearish market sentiment perhaps following the fewer number of working days from the previous week's auction. Quality of Ex-Estate offerings were barely maintained. Overall, a sharper decline for teas in the higher price bracket. Western BOP/BOPF's in the higher price bracket declined by up to Rs. 100 per kg and more for select high-priced teas. Most other teas on offer recorded a Rs. 20-40 per kg decline over the previous week's prices. The only exception would the Western High Grown 'BOP Special's' and 'PEK's' which were limited on offer and appreciated by up to Rs. 100 per kg. Low Growns totalled approximately 1.69 M/Kgs. Leafy, Semi-Leafy, Tippy and Premium categories met with fair demand. In the Leafy and Semi-Leafy catalogues, select BOP1's were firm, whilst the Best and Below Best together with the clean/bolder varieties were easier. Teas at the lower end maintained. High-priced OP1's were firm, whilst the Best, Below Best and teas at the bottom appreciated. OP's, in general, were lower. High-priced OPA's were easier, whilst the balance were irregular following quality. A few select Best PEK's appreciated on special inquiry, whilst the balance together with the Best were easier. commentary: The 4th Sale of the year concluded today had a total of 6.0 M/Kgs on offer. There was fair demand mostly at firm to easier rates. Ex-Estate offerings were barely maintained at 0.81 M/Kgs. Quality - A few select Westerns' showed improvement, whilst the others showed no significant change. Better demand for improved teas, whilst the others met with fair interest mostly selling around last week's levels. Improved teas were often sort after by interest emanating from shipper to the UK, the continent and perhaps South Africa, China and Japan. Less forceful buying from shippers to the CIS and Middle East, and consequently a bearish trend for teas at the lower end of the market. Accordingly, improved liquoring teas both in the Best and Below Best categories appreciated by Rs. 20-40 per kg, whilst the others were irregular and continued to sell around last week's levels. CTC teas, in general, too were weaker with only a few select invoices selling around last levels and the trend could be attributed to the slack demand from shippers to the CIS and Middle East, in general. Auction volumes in the forthcoming sale are likely to record a decline following current climatic conditions in the plantations which perhaps is not unusual for the time of year. Similarly, liquoring teas particularly from the Western slopes are seen to improve from Sale No. 5 onwards. Renewed interest for liquoring FBOP's, PEK's and PEK1's which were often selling between Rs. 1,400-1,600 per kg. Low Growns totalled

Liquoring Teas: Western High Grown BOP/BOPF's - Teas in the higher price bracket were irregular and sluggish partially following qualitative variances. Lighter/thinner liquoring teas notwithstanding an improved level of brightness failed to realise its value. Tea for price category, which at the commencement of the sale remained sluggish, gathered momentum as the sale progressed and sold around last week's levels with select coloury invoices appreciating by Rs. 20 per kg and more. Nuwara Eliyas - Limited interest and prices were barely maintained at its previous levels. Uda Pussellawas - Recorded a marginal appreciation in prices selectively though the price structure still continued at the lower end of the market. Uva - BOP's weakened by Rs. 20-40 per kg, whilst the BOPF's sold around last week's levels. High & Medium CTC teas - BP1's had hardly any offerings, whilst the PF1's continued to sell around last. Low Grown CTC teas - BP1's continued to maintain its previous momentum and sold at premium levels ranging between Rs. 1,300-1,400 per kg.
